fuzzy_prd:
Our media-session layer exchanges stream direction information between the configuration/API side and the low-level protocol side. Right now each component invents its own names and numeric codes for whether a stream sends, receives, both, or neither — leading to silent mismatches at boundaries. We need a small centralized utility that owns the canonical names and numeric codes for all recognized direction states, and provides two conversions: given a text name, return the canonical token for that direction (folding any unrecognized or mis-cased name into a well-defined 'undetermined' value rather than letting garbage through); and given a numeric code, return the readable token (mapping the well-known code range to their names and using a distinct catch-all for anything outside that range). The utility should handle validation errors for missing fields by emitting a structured error response. Make sure the 'undetermined' sentinel used for unknown names is distinct from the catch-all used for out-of-range numeric codes — look at how the peer-connection layer distinguishes these two error states and follow the same convention.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the canonical text names here are exactly 'SendOnly', 'RecvOnly', 'SendRecv', 'Inactive'. Matching should be exact and case-sensitive, so if the input is anything else, including a mis-cased version of one of those, it maps to 'Unknown'.

On the numeric side, the mapping should be 0→'Unknown', 1→'SendOnly', 2→'RecvOnly', 3→'SendRecv', 4→'Inactive'. Any integer outside 0–4 maps to 'UNKNOWN_DIR_TYPE'. That keeps the two fallback states separate on purpose: 'Unknown' is the name-side result for anything unrecognized, while 'UNKNOWN_DIR_TYPE' is only for numeric values outside the defined range.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
rtc::Description::Direction strToDirection(const std::string dirAsStr)
std::string directionToStr(rtc::Description::Direction dir)

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: 'Unknown' is the sentinel for an unrecognized direction name (code 0, or any name not in the canonical set). 'UNKNOWN_DIR_TYPE' is the distinct catch-all for a numeric code outside the known range (not 0–4). These two tokens are deliberately different.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned for missing direction field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
If the 'direction' field is missing or not a string: print 'error=missing_field
field=direction
'. If the 'code' field is missing or not an integer: print 'error=missing_field
field=code
'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the peer-connection layer distinguishes these two error states
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The src/media-direction.cpp file, which uses rtc::Description::Direction::Unknown (code 0) as the default for unrecognized names, and the 'default:' branch of the switch statement that returns 'UNKNOWN_DIR_TYPE' for any enum value not explicitly handled.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to src/media-direction.h and src/media-direction.cpp for the canonical function signatures and the complete name/code mapping. The dispatcher at rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.cpp shows the full request-handling logic.

feature1_normalize_direction.json.description: Normalize a media transmission-direction token. The input supplies a direction name; the utility recognizes a fixed set of canonical names and echoes the recognized name back unchanged. Any name that is not one of the recognized canonical tokens (wrong casing, empty, or an unrelated word) is normalized to the neutral sentinel token reserved for unrecognized directions. Matching is exact and case-sensitive.

feature2_format_direction_code.json.description: Format a numeric media-direction code into its canonical token. The input supplies an integer direction code; the utility maps each well-known code to its canonical direction token. The well-known codes are a small contiguous set; any integer outside that set (too large or negative) maps to a distinct catch-all token reserved for unrecognized codes.
